# Paper Summary: arXiv:0910.4793

**Title:** "Unbroken supersymmetry without new particles"

**Author:** Alejandro Rivero (Universidad de Zaragoza)

**Submission Details:**
- Submitted: October 26, 2009
- Revised: November 12, 2009
- Category: High Energy Physics - Phenomenology (hep-ph)

**Abstract:**
The paper examines "Koide's equation for charged leptons jointly with hypotheses implied in the ancient Dual Quark-Gluon model" with the goal of establishing motivation for a supersymmetric framework comparable to D=11 maximal supergravity.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Length: 7 pages (revised version)
- The work bridges particle physics phenomenology with mathematical relationships governing lepton mass hierarchies
- Explores theoretical frameworks that could accommodate supersymmetry without requiring discovery of new particle species

**Access:** PDF and TeX source files are available through arXiv's standard distribution channels.

# arXiv Paper Summary: 1111.7230

**Title:** "A possible origin of the q=4/3 diquark"

**Author:** Alejandro Rivero (BIFI, Universidad de Zaragoza)

**Submission Date:** November 14, 2011 (revised December 3, 2011)

**Classification:** High Energy Physics - Phenomenology (hep-ph)

## Abstract

The paper examines scalar diquarks with electric charge +4/3 proposed to explain the t-tbar asymmetry observed at the Tevatron. The research indicates that such diquarks are "also needed to classify all the scalars of the supersymmetric standard model, with three generations, under a global flavour symmetry."

## Key Details

- **Length:** 4 pages
- **DOI:** https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.1111.7230
- **PDF Available:** Yes (via arXiv repository)

The paper addresses theoretical physics concepts related to new particle physics candidates and supersymmetric extensions of the Standard Model, focusing on the theoretical framework needed to accommodate specific scalar particles within established symmetry constraints.

# arXiv Paper Summary: hep-ph/0606171

**Title:** "Mass terms to break susy-like degeneration"

**Author:** Alejandro Rivero

**Submission Date:** June 15, 2006

**Abstract:**
The researcher proposes "a very simple but general operator to break mass degeneration between representations of the Poincare group having spin 1 and 1/2." During this process, a quantity emerges that closely resembles Weinberg's angle, matching experimental values within 0.13 standard deviations.

**Document Details:**
- **Length:** 6 pages
- **Subject Classification:** High Energy Physics - Phenomenology (hep-ph)
- **arXiv Identifier:** hep-ph/0606171
- **DOI:** https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.hep-ph/0606171

**Available Formats:**
- PDF version
- TeX source code

No equations are explicitly displayed in the visible page content provided.

# Research Paper Summary

**Title:** "An interpretation of scalars in SO(32)"

**Author:** Alejandro Rivero

**Submission:** arXiv:2407.05397 (hep-ph), submitted July 7, 2024; last revised July 24, 2024

**Abstract:**

The paper proposes "an interpretation for the adjoint representation of the SO(32) group to classify the scalars of a generic Supersymmetric Standard Model having just three generations of particles, via a flavour group SU(5)." The researchers demonstrate this interpretation emerges from a self-consistency principle for scalar composites. Their model focuses exclusively on color and electric charge interactions while introducing "an additional chiral +4/3 quark per generation" as a consequence.

**Publication Status:** Published in European Physical Journal C 84, 1058 (2024)

**Key Details:**
- 8 pages with discussion on mass spectrum and partners (v3)
- DOI: 10.48550/arXiv.2407.05397
- Available as PDF, HTML, and TeX source

The research explores theoretical physics connections between group theory representations and particle physics phenomenology within supersymmetric extensions of the Standard Model.

# Document Summary

**Title:** "Possible Sensibility of Nuclear Fragmentation to the Mass of W"

**Author:** Alejandro Rivero

**Publication Details:** viXra:1509.0090, submitted September 7, 2015

**Abstract:** The paper examines "the possibility of an enhancement of the yields of asymmetric fission when the mass of one of the fragments has the same size that the electroweak W mass, causing some kind of recoil resonance."

**Additional Information:**
- Document type: 6-page draft note with graphical updates
- Format: PDF available
- Downloads: 182 unique-IP downloads
- Status: Pre-print repository (not peer-reviewed)

**Note:** No mathematical equations are visible in the displayed page content. The research appears to explore a potential relationship between nuclear fragmentation patterns and the W boson mass from electroweak physics.

# Paper Summary

**Title:** "Up to So(32) Via Supersymmetry "Bootstrap""

**Author:** Alejandro Rivero

**Abstract:** "A simple self-referencing postulate in the Chan-Paton factors of a string allows to fix some of the freedom in the landscape of SO(32) movel aspiring to be standard-model-like."

**Additional Information:**
- The submission consists of "three similar versions of 3-4 pages each, and a coda"
- Posted on viXra.org (a pre-print repository) on January 6, 2019
- Classification: High Energy Particle Physics
- Downloads: 199 unique-IP document downloads
- PDF available at: vixra:1901.0074v1

**Note:** No equations or additional key content beyond the abstract are visible on this page.

# Paper Summary

**Title:** "No Elementary Scalars in Experimental Supersymmetry"

**Author:** Alejandro Rivero

**viXra ID:** 1102.0034

**Submission Date:** February 20, 2011

**Abstract:**

The paper presents an interpretation of global SU(5) symmetry observed in scalar particles of the Supersymmetric Standard Model. The author proposes "that such scalars are actually the different aspects of the QCD string." Under this framework, only gauginos and potentially two neutral Higgs particles would be discoverable at the LHC.

**Document Details:**
- Length: 2 pages
- Downloads: 280 unique-IP downloads
- Format: PDF available

**Note:** This is a preprint on viXra.org, which hosts preliminary research not yet peer-reviewed. The disclaimer notes such articles "should be treated as preliminary."

# An Interpretation of Scalars in SO(32) - Complete Summary

## Overview
This paper by Alejandro Rivero proposes an interpretation of the SO(32) group's adjoint representation for classifying supersymmetric Standard Model scalars using an SU(5) flavor group framework.

## Key Contributions

### Main Proposal
The author interprets SO(32) decomposition as organizing scalars that could serve as supersymmetry partners to Standard Model fermions, specifically for three generations. The decomposition yields: "(1,24,1c) + [1,15,3̄c] + [1,15̄,3c]" representing the primary scalar content of interest.

### The "Turtles and Elephants" Postulate
A novel recursive composition principle is introduced where:
- Five "turtle" quarks (u, c, d, s, b) participate in scalar pair formation
- One "elephant" quark (top) does not participate in compositions
- This uniquely determines N=3 generations from self-consistency requirements

The mathematical constraints are:
- 2N = ku·kd (squark pairs)
- 2N = kd(kd+1)/2 (additional constraint)
- 4N = ku² + kd² - 1 (slepton consistency)

**Solution: N=3, ku=2, kd=3**

## Technical Framework

### Hypercharge Construction
Two hypercharges (Y₁, Y₂) combine to produce electric charge:
"Q = (1/5)(2Y₁/3 - Y₂)"

This enables recovery of Standard Model particle charges including the problematic +4/3 scalar quarks from the (1,3) representation.

### Group Decomposition Chain
SO(32) → SU(16)×U(1) → SU(15)×U(1) → SU(5)×SU(3)

The resulting multiplet structure accommodates three generations with explicit color and electric charge assignments.

## Mass Formula Application

The author applies a classical preon mass formula:
"E(qa,qb) = (qa+qb)²KΩ"

This generates paired scalar masses required by supersymmetry, with solutions parameterized by angle α. Notably, α=0 produces exact mass pairs across multiplets, while α=0.745821 recovers lepton mass ratios me:mμ:mτ.

## Notable Results

**Table findings at α=0:**
- Squark masses exhibit pairing structures
- Factor-of-three relationships emerge between quark and lepton spectra
- Additional constraint: ∑mq = 3∑mℓ holds empirically for (ms,mc,mb) vs (me,mμ,mτ)

## Conceptual Innovation

Rather than assuming fermion-scalar duality, this work proposes scalars are composites of fermion pairs while fermions remain elementary. This resolves why superpartners haven't been observed—"supersymmetry could be hiding in plain sight, not broken but distorted."

## Additional Explorations

The paper discusses SO(30), SU(15), SU(8), and E₈×E₈ as related alternatives, though none achieve the same elegance for the three-generation constraint. The E₈ case proves particularly problematic, as factorizations decompose too quickly to accommodate the 15-dimensional SU(5) representation.

## Outstanding Issues

- The +4/3 scalar quarks lack clear phenomenological interpretation
- Connection to dynamical binding mechanisms remains unspecified
- Chirality treatment of Weyl-type scalars incomplete
- Missing symmetry preventing comprehensive mass formula across all generations

## Conclusion

This work presents an unconventional but mathematically consistent framework linking SO(32) group theory to observable Standard Model structure through composite scalar interpretation, with generation number uniquely determined by self-consistency postulates.

# A Possible Origin of the q=4/3 Diquark

## Complete Paper Summary

### Abstract
The paper proposes that scalar diquarks with electric charge +4/3, needed to explain the t​t̄ asymmetry at the Tevatron, naturally emerge from imposing flavor symmetry on the supersymmetric standard model's scalar sector rather than on fermions.

### Section 1: Introduction
The author discusses forward-backward asymmetry measurements in top quark processes and notes that "some scalar 'diquarks' in diverse multiplets are favoured" to explain the anomaly. The novel contribution is demonstrating these particles appear uniquely in a flavor-symmetric construction of supersymmetric scalars with three generations.

### Section 2: Flavour in SUSY Scalars
A crucial construction uses SU(5) flavor symmetry. The 24 sleptons decompose as:
**24 = (1,1) + (3,1) + (2,3) + (2,3̄) + (1,8)**

For squarks, using 5̄ ⊗ 5̄ = 15 ⊕ 10:
**15 = (3,1) + (2,3) + (1,6)**

The (3,1) triplet contains the charge +4/3 scalars interpretable as diquarks. The author notes this "supersymmetric Bootstrap" becomes "specially elegant—and unique in some terms—when done for three" generations.

### Section 3: Mass Spectrum
Speculation that SUSY flavor symmetry based on composites could yield mass formulae. References the Koide equation and suggests connections to earlier texture models and permutation symmetries from the 1970s-1980s.

### Section 4: Towards Technicolor
Proposes that charge +4/3 diquarks might acquire partial charge from B−L via non-chiral interactions in left-right symmetric GUT models. Suggests these could relate to scalar components of gauge supermultiplets relevant to electroweak symmetry breaking.

### References
Fourteen citations spanning theoretical work on the Tevatron asymmetry, composite models, mass relations, and grand unification.

**Key mathematical content:** Mostly SU(5) representation decompositions and charge assignments under branching to SU(3) × SU(2) subgroups.

# Unbroken Supersymmetry, Without New Particles

## Full Text Reconstruction

**Title:** Unbroken supersymmetry, without new particles

**Author:** Alejandro Rivero, Institute for Biocomputation and Physics of Complex Systems, University of Zaragoza, Spain

**Abstract:**
The paper examines "Koide's equation for charged leptons jointly with the hypotheses implied in the ancient Dual Quark-Gluon model" with focus on motivating a supersymmetric framework resembling D=11 maximal supergravity.

---

## I. Koide's Model of Subquarks

The author discusses Y. Koide's 1981 proposal involving composite quarks and leptons from elementary bosons and fermions with SU(3) color and generational symmetry. Koide's symmetry breaking scheme constrains the square root of the lepton mass matrix M_l:

**(U+V)² = M_l**

Where U represents the central component and V represents the traceless part, satisfying:

**Tr[U²] − Tr[V²] = 0**

The paper notes this relationship holds with PDG 2009 data to remarkable precision: "the quotient Tr[U²]/Tr[V²] is 1.00002±0.00008."

Two key observations:
1. The condition on mass's square root "smells to a condition on supersymmetry generators"
2. If supersymmetry were unbroken, this constraint should appear in scalar partners of charged fermions

---

## II. sBootstrap

The author proposes that "sfermion particles are really quarks themselves." With five light quarks and one massive quark, the flavor global symmetry is SU(5), decomposable as SU(3)×SU(2).

**Central thesis:** For three generations with one massive quark, "the system closes on itself: the degrees of freedom generated in the product are the ones needed for the sfermions of a supersymmetric standard model."

The paper states: "It is not possible to do the same trick with any other number of generations, so in some sense the sBootstrap fixes the number of generations."

---

## III. D=11 Supergravity Multiplet

The author identifies seven extra dimensions as minimum for SU(3)×SU(2)×U(1) symmetry. D=11 supergravity's minimal multiplet contains 128-component fermions requiring additional fields to accommodate 84 bosonic degrees of freedom.

Numerological observations:
- "Charged fermions" of the standard model = 84 degrees of freedom (plus 12 neutrinos = 96)
- "Light fermions" = 84 degrees of freedom (plus 3 colors of top quark = 96)

The paper discusses Type IIA strings recovering 11D supergravity in strong coupling limits, with decompositions showing 28 in NS-NS and 56 in R-R sectors.

---

## IV. Koide's Relationship for Mesons

Examining mass relationships in meson sectors, the author finds that "Koide's relationship fares better" when setting bottom and strange quark masses equal to down quark mass. The quotient Tr[U²]/Tr[V²] achieves approximately 1.005 in this configuration.

The paper notes: "It seems that the breaking of SUSY aims to preserve Koide's, contrary to our initial expectation."

---

## V. Puzzling Chirality

The author addresses the fundamental problem that "there are no chiral fermions in D=11, thus no way to implement SU(2) in the way it works in the standard model."

The paper suggests the electroweak SU(2)×U(1) "interpolates, as we move the mass of W from zero to infinity, between two non-chiral theories in D=9 and D=11."

The (3,1) triplet representation from SU(5) decomposition presents theoretical challenges, as particles "should have electric charge 4/3" and accounting problems with color and chiral structure.

---

## References

The paper cites 9 references including:
- Koide's foundational 1981 work on composite quarks and leptons
- Neveu-Schwarz and Ramond's early superstring contributions
- Contemporary discussions of supergravity and geometric approaches to the standard model

# Mass Terms to Break SUSY-like Degeneration

## Author
Alejandro Rivero (EUPT, University of Zaragoza, Spain)

## Abstract
The paper proposes an operator to break mass degeneracy between Poincaré group representations with spin 1 and 1/2. A quantity resembling Weinberg's angle emerges from this process at the 0.13σ experimental precision level.

## Section 1: Mass Terms from Casimir Invariants

The author seeks mass operators $M_s^2$ constructed from Poincaré Casimir invariants $\mathcal{C}_1$ and $\mathcal{C}_2$ with eigenvalues $c_1 = m^2$ and $c_2 = -m^2s(s+1)$.

**Key constraint:** The asymptotic condition requires $\lim_{s\to\infty}m_s^2 = m$, preserving Regge trajectories from string theory.

The simplest solution satisfying this condition emerges from:
$$M_s^4 - M_s^2\mathcal{C}_2 + \mathcal{C}_1\mathcal{C}_2 = 0$$

This can be expressed using Pauli matrices to avoid square roots:
$$M_s^2 = \sigma^+ \otimes \mathcal{C}_1\mathcal{C}_2 + \sigma^- \otimes \mathbf{I} + \frac{\mathbf{I}-\sigma_z}{2} \otimes \mathcal{C}_2$$

## Central Finding: de Vries' Angle

Hans de Vries discovered that positive eigenvalues for $s=1/2$ and $s=1$ yield:
$$s_{dV}^2 \equiv 1 - \frac{m_{s=1/2,+}^2}{m_{s=1,+}^2} = 0.22310132...$$

This remarkably approaches the electroweak Weinberg angle:
$$s_W^2 = 1 - \frac{M_W^2}{M_Z^2} = 0.22306 \pm 0.00033$$

The ratio between these values is $s_{dV}^2/s_W^2 = 0.9998 \pm 0.0015$, representing exceptional agreement for a tree-level prediction.

## Section 2: Breaking SUSY Multiplet Degeneracy

For degenerate mass $(m,s_i)$ representations (like SUSY multiplets), the operator:
$$M_{(s)}^2 = \frac{1}{2}\left(\mathcal{C}_2 + \sqrt{(\mathcal{C}_2)^2 - 4\mathcal{C}_1\mathcal{C}_2}\right)$$

breaks degeneracy while maintaining asymptotic Regge behavior.

## Predictions from Z Boson Mass Input

Using measured $M_Z^2 = (91.1874\text{ GeV})^2$:

- $m_{s=1/2,+}^2 = (80.3717\text{ GeV})^2$ (near $M_W$)
- $m_{s=1,-}^2 = -(176.154\text{ GeV})^2$
- $m_{s=1/2,-}^2 = -(122.384\text{ GeV})^2$

The negative eigenvalue for spin-1 aligns with electroweak symmetry breaking: the vacuum expectation value $\langle v \rangle/\sqrt{2} = 174.1042 \pm 0.00075$ GeV matches predictions with coupling $\lambda_h \approx 1$.

## Section 3: De Broglie's 1924 Relativistic Quantum Mechanics

De Vries combined de Broglie's relativistic orbit rule with the Landé-Pauli angular momentum substitution. Setting orbital period $T_r = h/(m_0c^2)$ yields:
$$\frac{\beta^2}{\sqrt{1-\beta^2}} = \sqrt{j(j+1)}$$

For $j=1/2$ and $j=1$:
$$\beta_{1/2} = \sqrt{\frac{3}{8}(\sqrt{19/3}-1)} = 0.7541414...$$
$$\beta_1 = \sqrt{\sqrt{3}-1} = 0.8555996...$$

The dimensionless ratio produces:
$$s_{dV}^2 \equiv 1 - \left(\frac{\beta_{1/2}}{\beta_1}\right)^2 = 0.22310132...$$

This historical connection links early quantum mechanics to modern electroweak physics.

## Implications

The agreement suggests the electroweak scale may be defined as the point where the running Weinberg angle reaches de Vries' value. The top Yukawa coupling $\lambda_t = 0.991 \pm 0.013$ aligns with these calculations, potentially indicating stringy properties in electroweak symmetry breaking mechanisms.

## Additional Numerical Coincidences

The author notes other near-matches involving the Weinberg angle:
- $\cos\theta = \sinh^{-1}(1) - s_W^2 = 0.2231806$
- $\sin\theta/\cos\theta = \beta_1^4 - s_W^2 = 0.223112151$

Though interesting, these lack clear physical interpretation compared to the primary de Vries result.

# Complete Text: "An interpretation of scalars in SO(32)"

## Abstract

The paper proposes that "the adjoint representation of the SO(32) group classifies the scalars of a generic Supersymmetric Standard Model having just three generations of particles, via a flavour group SU(5)."

## I. Introduction

The SO(32) group, though relevant in string theory and supergravity, lacks complex representations making it unsuitable as a unification group. However, when decomposed, it provides an interesting family structure for scalars that could serve as supersymmetry partners of Standard Model fermions.

## II. The Flavour Group in SO(32)

The adjoint representation decomposes as:

496 = (1,24,1^c) + [1,15,3̄^c] + [1,1̄5,3^c] + (1,24,8^c) + [1,10,6̄^c] + [1,1̄0,6^c] + (1,1,8^c) + (2,5,3^c) + (2,5̄,3̄^c) + (1,1,1^c) + [1,1,1^c]

The three boldfaced components are the primary focus, with explicit hypercharge assignments Y₁ = 0, +1, -1.

### SU(5) Decomposition

The 15-plet and 24-plet decompose under SU(5) → SU(3)×SU(2)×U₂(1):

15 = (1,3)₋₆ + (3,2)₋₁ + (6,1)₄

24 = (1,1)₀ + (1,3)₀ + (3,2)₅ + (3̄,2)₋₅ + (8,1)₀

### Electric Charge Formula

The charge assignment becomes:

Q = (1/5)(2Y₁/3 - Y₂)

This produces scalar content matching a three-generation supersymmetric standard model with color and electric charge quantum numbers.

## III. SO(32) from Postulates

### III.1 Turtles and Elephants Framework

The paper introduces a novel concept: scalars as composites of quark pairs, with quarks divided into "turtles" (binding-capable) and "elephants" (non-binding).

For N total generations with k_u up-type and k_d down-type "turtles," the consistency conditions are:

2N = k_u k_d                    (8)

2N = k_d(k_d + 1)/2             (9)

Adding conditions for sleptons:

2N = k_u k_d                    (10)

4N = k_u² + k_d² - 1           (11)

**Unique solution:** N = 3, k_u = 2, k_d = 3

This yields "five turtles and one elephant," identified as the top quark.

### III.2 Flavour Groups

Without color, flavor organizes into SO(10)'s 54-plet:

54 = 15₄ + 1̄5₋₄ + 24₀

Color-flavor unification suggests SU(15) as the minimal candidate, decomposing via SU(15) ⊃ SU(5)×SU(3).

## IV. One Generation Case (Georgi-Glashow Model)

The SU(5) representations decompose as:

5 = (1,2)₋₃ + (3,1)₂

10 = (1,1)₋₆ + (3̄,1)₄ + (3,2)₋₁

The union 10 + 1̄0 + 24 represents the single-generation version, with crucial +4/3 scalar quarks appearing naturally.

## V. The Role of the Top

The top quark's identification as the non-binding "elephant" derives from horizontal flavor symmetries SU(3)_{f₃}×SU(2)_{f₂}, where the f₂ symmetry relates u and c quarks, excluding t. The top's heavy mass, approaching the Fermi scale, prevents traditional binding.

## VI. Discussion on Masses

### Mass Formula

A classical approach assumes all mass derives from an abelian charge, with energy for a pair (q_a, q_b):

E(q_a, q_b) = (q_a + q_b)²K_Ω     (14)

For three-preon composites, two conditions ensure realistic masses:

z₁ + z₂ + z₃ = 0               (15)

z₀² = (z₁² + z₂² + z₃²)/3       (16)

### Angular Parametrization

Using angle α to parametrize abelian charges from SU(3):

z₁ = (1/2)cos α + (1/2√3)sin α
z₂ = 0 - (1/√3)sin α
z₃ = -(1/2)cos α + (1/2√3)sin α
z₀ = ±1/√6

For α = 0.745821, the formula recovers exactly m_e, m_μ, m_τ using k = m_e + m_μ + m_τ.

### Mass Tables

**Table 1** presents "slepton" masses for various α values, showing pairs of equal masses in (3,2) representations.

**Table 2** presents "squark" masses, demonstrating mass pairing needed for unbroken supersymmetry.

### Paired Scalars Analysis

For α = 0, exact mass pairs appear throughout all representations. For α = π/2 or π/6, some representations show two equal masses with a different third value.

The discrepancy analysis suggests an isospin exchange structure:
u = c = 313.85, s = 0, d = b = 470.8

Producing:
(u,c,t) = (0, 470.8, 1883)
(d,s,b) = (15.8, 313.85, 1553.4)

## VII. Related Groups

### VII.1 SU(15)

Previous authors embedded full generations in SU(15)'s fundamental representation. This work instead uses 105, 1̄05, and 224 representations for the five "turtle" quarks (u,c,d,s,b).

### VII.2 SU(8)

The group decomposes via SO(16):

496 = 1₀ + 120₄ + 1̄20₋₄ + 255₀

Further breaking to SU(5)⊗SU(3)⊗U(1) provides alternative hypercharge assignments, though lacks the "recursive" color properties of SO(32).

The 28-plet under 8⊃SU(5)⊗SU(3):

28 = (1,3̄)₋₁₀ + (5,3)₋₂ + (10,1)₆

### VII.3 E₈⊗E₈

While relevant to 10D supergravity, E₈ factorizations fail to produce representations as large as SU(5)'s 15-plet. Direct branching E8 ⊃ SU(5)⊗SU(5) reduces higher multiplets too quickly, though alternative charge assignments could accommodate ±4/3 quarks.

## VIII. Discussion

The postulate "It is turtles all the way down" uniquely fixes three generations. While the model accounts for most multiplets, the ±4/3 scalar quarks remain puzzling—they cannot organize as Dirac partners of four-component quarks but play essential roles in flavor multiplets.

The composite scalars differ from QCD-scale diquarks and mesons, suggesting operation at higher energy scales. The binding mechanism resembles open string terminations rather than conventional hadronic binding.

## IX. Conclusions

Key findings:

1. SO(32)'s adjoint representation naturally accommodates three-generation supersymmetric standard model scalars
2. A self-consistency postulate about composite scalar formation uniquely determines N=3
3. The decomposition identifies five "light" quarks and one heavy (top) quark with distinct roles
4. Classical mass formulas can produce equal-mass pairs required by unbroken supersymmetry
5. The ±4/3 scalar quarks represent phenomenologically exotic but theoretically necessary content

The work suggests supersymmetry "could be hiding in plain sight, not broken but distorted," with elementary fermions and composite scalars.

# Complete Text of "A possible origin of the q=4/3 diquark"

## Title and Author
**A possible origin of the q=4/3 diquark**

A. Rivero, Institute for Biocomputation and Physics of Complex Systems, University of Zaragoza
(al.rivero@gmail.com, arivero@unizar.es)

## Abstract

"Between the new physics candidates proposed to explain the tt̄ asymmetry measured in the Tevatron, there are some scalar diquarks with electric charge +4/3."

The paper notes these diquarks are also needed to classify scalars of the supersymmetric standard model with three generations under global flavour symmetry.

## 1 Introduction

The forward-backward asymmetry of top quark processes at Tevatron has generated significant interest. Among proposed New Physics candidates, scalar "diquarks" in diverse multiplets appear favored, particularly isosinglets with charge 4/3.

The author proposes that when flavour symmetry is applied to the scalar sector (squarks and sleptons) rather than standard model fermions, these candidate particles emerge from a unique construction that exhausts exactly for three generations with five light quarks.

## 2 Flavour in susy scalars

A flavour SU(5) symmetry can classify the 24 sleptons in the SSM extended with right neutrinos using:

$$5 \otimes \bar{5} = 24 \oplus 1$$

Branching to SU(3) × SU(2):

$$24 = (1,1) + (3,1) + (2,3) + (2,\bar{3}) + (1,8)$$

Assigning electric charge +2/3 to the SU(3) piece and −1/3 to SU(2) yields a sextet of charge +1, an opposite sextet of charge −1, and twelve zero-charge states.

For squarks, the construction uses:

$$\bar{5} \otimes \bar{5} = 15 \oplus 10$$

Branching yields:

$$15 = (3,1) + (2,3) + (1,6)$$

This produces one sextet of charge −1/3 and another of charge +2/3. The author terms this "supersymmetric Bootstrap" (sBootstrap).

The SU(3) piece can be interpreted as coming from quarks d, s, b and the SU(2) piece from u, c. The construction becomes uniquely elegant with three generations—additional generations would require exotic squarks and sleptons.

The 15 multiplet contains three extra scalars not in the standard model. Being odd in number, they cannot be arranged in Dirac supermultiplets. Importantly, the (3,1) triplet consists of three scalars with charge +4/3 that can be assigned as scalar diquark interactions—"the same kind of particle expected to solve the Tevatron asymmetry."

The author notes this can be read bidirectionally: either extra flavour symmetry in SUSY's scalar sector predicts 4/3 diquarks, or models explaining the asymmetry may exhibit explicit scalar flavour symmetry when supersymmetrized.

## 3 A mass spectrum

The section explores whether SUSY with its own flavour symmetry based on composites permits a mass formula. Remnants might persist in the fermion sector after SUSY breaking.

The discussion references the Koide equation from 2005 and its applications to neutrino spectra. Mass formulas and Cabibbo angle determinations from the late 1970s used textures, democratic matrices, or permutation symmetry in SU(2)_R ⊗ SU(2)_L models.

Recent empirical work extended the Koide formula from leptons to quarks. The m_e = m_u = 0 spectrum might serve as a starting point for supersymmetrical spectrum searches. Left-right models relating the bottom R quark to the up L quark suggest superpartners might be positioned at the b quark's energy.

## 4 Towards technicolor

The section explores exploiting these diquarks in Higgs-like scenarios. The peculiar charge of +4/3 presents an impediment, but an interesting possibility emerges: some charge comes from B−L in a non-chiral way, typical of GUT models using left-right symmetry.

The (3,1) diquarks should be viewed as having non-chiral interactions providing colour and:

$$Q_v = \frac{1}{2}(B-L) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

of electric charge, plus chiral interaction providing Q_ch = +1. Condensation might eliminate the vector interaction component, making diquarks resemble gauge supermultiplet scalars: colour-neutral with integer electric charge.

Treating B-L differently than other symmetries also suggests increased skepticism toward R-symmetry. The produced scalars have B-L values differing by one unit from their fermion partners.

Colour neutrality causes degeneracy, reducing different states to three particles and antiparticles. Under SUSY, massless gauge supermultiplets require eating one chiral supermultiplet to gain mass; one scalar becomes the W or Z extra degree of freedom, the other surfaces as Higgs. With colour neutrality providing appropriate scalar numbers, three chiral supermultiplets could be formed.

## 5 Acknowledgements

The author thanks Manuel Perez Victoria, Nejc Kosnic, and Ilja Doršner for updated information about top asymmetry research and convention clarifications. Encouragement regarding sBootstrap divulgation came from Tommaso Dorigo, Mitchell Porter, and PhysicsForums.com mentors.

## References

[Complete reference list as provided in the source document, containing 14 citations from 1981-2011]

# Complete Text of "An Interpretation of Scalars in SO(32)"

## Abstract

The paper proposes interpreting the adjoint representation of SO(32) to classify scalars in a Supersymmetric Standard Model with three generations through an SU(5) flavor group. The authors demonstrate this interpretation emerges from self-consistency postulates for composite scalars, requiring only color and electric charge while introducing an additional +4/3 quark per generation.

## 1. Introduction

While SO(32) lacks complex representations unsuitable for unification, its decomposition yields interesting flavor structure. The paper reviews this decomposition and presents a scalar interpretation, then reconstructs scalars as composites. This reconstruction constrains the generation count and may apply to open string theory or composite model foundations.

## 2. The Flavour Group in SO(32)

### Group Decomposition

For SO(2n) groups with explicit SU(3) color:
$$SO(n_1) \otimes SU(n_2) \otimes SU(3) \otimes U_1(1)$$
with constraint: $2n = n_1 + 6n_2$

For SO(32) with maximal $n_2 = 5$, the adjoint decomposes as 496 components:

$$(1,24,1^c) + [1,15,\bar{3}^c] + [1,\bar{15},3^c] + \text{(other terms)}$$

The three bold terms provide scalars matching a minimal three-generation supersymmetric model.

### Hypercharge Construction

SU(5) flavor decomposes under SU(3) × SU(2) × U₂(1):

$$15 = (1,3)_{-6} + (3,2)_{-1} + (6,1)_4$$

$$24 = (1,1)_0 + (1,3)_0 + (3,2)_5 + (\bar{3},2)_{-5} + (8,1)_0$$

Electric charge formula:
$$Q = \frac{1}{5}\left(\frac{2}{3}Y_1 - Y_2\right)$$

This yields Standard Model scalar content including squarks with charges ±1/3, ±2/3 and sleptons.

### Decomposition Chain

Alternative path via:
$$SO(32) \supset SU(16) \times U(1) \supset SU(15) \times U(1) \supset SU(5) \times SU(3)$$

with: $496 = 1_0 + 120_4 + \bar{120}_{-4} + 255_0$

## 3. SO(32) from Postulates

### 3.1 Turtles and Elephants

The paper introduces a novel framework treating scalars as composites of quark pairs. Quarks divide into two classes:
- **Turtles**: can combine into composites
- **Elephants**: cannot combine

With N generations, ku turtles of up-type and kd turtles of down-type, supersymmetry requires:

$$2N = k_u k_d$$

$$2N = \frac{k_d(k_d+1)}{2}$$

$$4N = k_u^2 + k_d^2 - 1$$

Unique solution: $N = 3$, $k_u = 2$, $k_d = 3$

This yields five light "turtle" quarks and one heavy "elephant" (the top quark) that doesn't participate in composites.

**Additional result**: Three extra scalar quarks of charge ±4/3 emerge from all turtle combinations.

### 3.2 Colourless and Coloured Flavour Groups

Without color, flavor organizes in SO(10) adjoint (54 representation):

$$54 = 15_4 + \bar{15}_{-4} + 24_0$$

The additional ±4/3 scalar quarks complete the SU(5) multiplet structure. Minimal colored-flavor unification uses SU(15), which can embed into SO(30) or SO(32).

## 4. A Case for One Generation

The Georgi-Glashow SU(5) model decomposes as:

$$5 = (1,2)_{-3} + (3,1)_2$$

$$10 = (1,1)_{-6} + (\bar{3},1)_4 + (3,2)_{-1}$$

The union $10 + \bar{10} + 24$ represents a single-generation version of the three-generation construction, with X bosons appearing as +4/3 scalars. The framework constrains both fermion and scalar content precisely.

## 5. Discussion on Related Groups

### 5.1 On SU(15)

SU(15) was previously proposed as a GUT group. Unlike traditional approaches embedding complete generations in the fundamental representation, this paper uses:

$$(u_{rgb}, c_{rgb}, d_{rgb}, s_{rgb}, b_{rgb})$$

with SU(5) flavor turtles and representations 105, $\bar{105}$, 224 of SU(15).

Recent work by Corianò and Frampton considers SU(15) with bifermion extensions, maintaining leptons in fundamental representations and preserving lepton number conservation.

### 5.2 On SU(8)

Alternative SO(32) path via:
$$SU(16) \supset SO(16) \supset SU(8) \times U(1)$$

The adjoint 496 branches as:
$$496 = 1 + 3(1_0 + 28_2 + \bar{28}_{-2} + 63_0) + 36_2 + \bar{36}_{-2} + 63_0$$

SU(8) decomposes under SU(5) × SU(3) × U(1). The 28 representation contains quark-like content:

$$28 = (1,\bar{3})_{-10} + (5,3)_{-2} + (10,1)_6$$

However, this lacks the "recursive" color property enabling the three-generation constraint found in SO(32).

### 5.3 On E₈ × E₈

E₈ branches to SO(16) as:
$$248 = (120) \oplus (128')$$

Both SO(32) and E₈ × E₈ contain SO(16) × SO(16) subgroup. However, factorizations of SO(16) fail to produce representations as large as the 15 of SU(5), falling to smaller representations too quickly. This prevents easy application of the generation-fixing postulates.

The branching $E_8 \supset SU(5) \otimes SU(5)$ produces only 5 and 10 representations, requiring exchange of ±4/3 and ±2/3 charges for excess ±1/3 content.

## 6. Discussion

### Key Observations

The "turtles all the way down" postulate uniquely determines three generations when combined with reasonable conditions on neutral slepton construction. While SO(32) decomposition doesn't explicitly show this uniqueness, the framework identifies five "turtles" and one "elephant."

The mysterious ±4/3 scalar quarks cannot organize as three generations of Dirac partners but serve essential roles in the SU(5) flavor multiplet structure.

### Connection to Composite Models

The results relate to Technicolor (TC) and Extended Technicolor (ETC) approaches. The top quark's special status parallels its early disintegration preventing meson binding. Scalar-composite binding occurs at higher energy scales than typical hadron physics, with masses comparable to Standard Model fermions—consistent with slightly broken supersymmetry.

The composite interpretation suggests terminated open string endpoints labeled by quark quantum numbers, similar to planar orientifold techniques in string theory.

### Differences from Preon Models

Unlike preon constructions using identical groups, this approach focuses on scalar pairs derived from fermions, naturally explaining scalar-fermion pairing in supersymmetry. Recovering fermions would require additional spin-1/2 contributions (particles or strings).

## 7. Conclusions

The paper presents a novel SO(32) interpretation emphasizing its role as a flavor group in supersymmetric contexts. Key contributions:

1. **Novel decomposition**: The hypercharge assignment recovering three generations for scalars hadn't appeared explicitly in literature, likely because previous work targeted fermions rather than scalars.

2. **Avoids chiral problem**: Focusing on scalars sidesteps SO(32)'s well-known deficit of chiral fermion representations.

3. **Generation constraint**: A composite postulate for supersymmetric scalars uniquely fixes three generations and distinguishes five light quarks from one heavy ("elephant") quark.

4. **Exotic but motivated**: While unconventional—proposing scalar composites from SM fermions—this framework reproduces SO(32) decomposition and validates the three-generation structure.

5. **Unresolved issues**: Each generation includes two extra ±4/3 scalar quarks. Their associated fermion partners remain unclear, as they would necessarily be Weyl rather than Dirac spinors.

## 8. Acknowledgements

Work supported by Spanish MCIN/AEI project PID2022-136374NB-C22 and European Union funding. Figure credit: Álvaro de Rújula. Discussion contributions: M. Porter.

# Paper 1: arXiv:0910.4793

**Title:** "Unbroken supersymmetry without new particles"
**Author:** Alejandro Rivero (BIFI / Institute for Biocomputation and Physics of Complex Systems, Universidad de Zaragoza)
**Submitted:** October 26, 2009 (v1); November 12, 2009 (v2)
**Category:** hep-ph, 7 pages
**DOI:** https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.0910.4793

---

## Abstract

"We consider Koide's equation for charged leptons jointly with hypotheses implied in the ancient Dual Quark-Gluon model. The focus is to motivate the possibility of a supersymmetric framework akin to the one of D=11 maximal supergravity."

---

## Section Structure

- I. Koide's Model of Subquarks
- II. sBootstrap
- III. D=11 Supergravity Multiplet
- IV. Koide's Relationship for Mesons
- V. Puzzling Chirality
- References (9 citations)

---

## Section I: Koide's Model of Subquarks

Y. Koide's 1981 proposal involves composite quarks and leptons from elementary bosons and fermions with SU(3) color and generational symmetry. Koide's symmetry-breaking scheme constrains the square root of the lepton mass matrix M_l:

```latex
(U + V)^2 = M_l
```

where U is the central component and V the traceless part, satisfying:

```latex
\mathrm{Tr}[U^2] - \mathrm{Tr}[V^2] = 0
```

With PDG 2009 data the quotient $\mathrm{Tr}[U^2]/\mathrm{Tr}[V^2] = 1.00002 \pm 0.00008$.

Two key observations are made:
1. The condition on mass's square root "smells to a condition on supersymmetry generators."
2. If supersymmetry were unbroken this constraint should appear in scalar partners of charged fermions.

---

## Section II: sBootstrap

The author proposes that "sfermion particles are really quarks themselves." With five light quarks and one massive quark, the flavor global symmetry is SU(5), decomposable as SU(3) x SU(2).

**Central thesis:** For three generations with one massive quark, "the system closes on itself: the degrees of freedom generated in the product are the ones needed for the sfermions of a supersymmetric standard model."

"It is not possible to do the same trick with any other number of generations, so in some sense the sBootstrap fixes the number of generations."

---

## Section III: D=11 Supergravity Multiplet

Seven extra dimensions are identified as the minimum for SU(3) x SU(2) x U(1) symmetry. D=11 supergravity's minimal multiplet contains 128-component fermions requiring additional fields to accommodate 84 bosonic degrees of freedom.

Numerological observations:
- "Charged fermions" of the standard model = 84 degrees of freedom (plus 12 neutrinos = 96)
- "Light fermions" = 84 degrees of freedom (plus 3 colors of top quark = 96)

Type IIA strings recover 11D supergravity in strong coupling limits, with decompositions showing 28 in NS-NS and 56 in R-R sectors.

---

## Section IV: Koide's Relationship for Mesons

Examining meson mass sectors, the author finds Koide's relationship "fares better" when setting bottom and strange quark masses equal to down quark mass. The quotient $\mathrm{Tr}[U^2]/\mathrm{Tr}[V^2]$ achieves approximately 1.005 in this configuration.

"It seems that the breaking of SUSY aims to preserve Koide's, contrary to our initial expectation."

---

## Section V: Puzzling Chirality

"There are no chiral fermions in D=11, thus no way to implement SU(2) in the way it works in the standard model."

The electroweak SU(2) x U(1) "interpolates, as we move the mass of W from zero to infinity, between two non-chiral theories in D=9 and D=11."

The (3,1) triplet representation from SU(5) decomposition produces particles that "should have electric charge 4/3."

---

## Main Results/Conclusions

- The sBootstrap (sfermions = quarks) closes uniquely for 3 generations with 5 light + 1 heavy quark.
- This matches the D=11 supergravity degree-of-freedom count.
- Koide's equation holds with precision $10^{-5}$ for charged leptons.
- The framework suggests unbroken SUSY is hiding in plain sight within known particles, without new superpartners.

---

---

# Paper 2: arXiv:1111.7230

**Title:** "A possible origin of the q=4/3 diquark"
**Author:** Alejandro Rivero (BIFI, Universidad de Zaragoza)
**Submitted:** November 14, 2011 (v1); December 3, 2011 (v2)
**Category:** hep-ph, 4 pages
**DOI:** https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.1111.7230

---

## Abstract

"Between the new physics candidates proposed to explain the tt̄ asymmetry measured in the Tevatron, there are some scalar diquarks with electric charge +4/3." These are also needed to classify all scalars of the supersymmetric standard model with three generations under global flavour symmetry.

---

## Section Structure

1. Introduction
2. Flavour in SUSY Scalars
3. A Mass Spectrum
4. Towards Technicolor
5. Acknowledgements
6. References (14 citations)

---

## Section 1: Introduction

The forward-backward t-tbar asymmetry at the Tevatron motivates new physics. Among candidates, scalar diquarks with charge +4/3 are favored. The paper proposes these emerge uniquely from a flavour-symmetric construction of SUSY scalars with exactly 3 generations and 5 light quarks.

---

## Section 2: Flavour in SUSY Scalars

A flavour SU(5) symmetry classifies the 24 sleptons in the SSM (with right-handed neutrinos):

```latex
5 \otimes \bar{5} = 24 \oplus 1
```

Branching to SU(3) x SU(2):

```latex
24 = (1,1) + (3,1) + (2,3) + (2,\bar{3}) + (1,8)
```

Assigning electric charge +2/3 to the SU(3) piece and -1/3 to SU(2) gives sextets of charge +1, -1, and twelve charge-zero states.

For squarks:

```latex
\bar{5} \otimes \bar{5} = 15 \oplus 10
```

with:

```latex
15 = (3,1) + (2,3) + (1,6)
```

This gives one sextet of charge -1/3 and one of charge +2/3. The (3,1) triplet consists of three scalars with charge **+4/3** -- "the same kind of particle expected to solve the Tevatron asymmetry."

The SU(3) piece of the 24 comes from quarks (d, s, b) and the SU(2) piece from (u, c). The construction is unique and exhausts exactly for three generations.

The 15 multiplet contains three extra scalars not in the standard model. Being odd in number they cannot be arranged in Dirac supermultiplets.

---

## Section 3: A Mass Spectrum

References the Koide equation and connections to earlier texture/permutation symmetry models. The $m_e = m_u = 0$ spectrum might serve as a starting point for supersymmetric spectrum searches. Left-right models suggest superpartners might be positioned at the b quark's energy scale.

---

## Section 4: Towards Technicolor

The diquarks have charge $Q_v = \frac{1}{2}(B-L) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$ from non-chiral B-L interaction, plus chiral $Q_{ch} = +1$.

Condensation might eliminate the vector component, making diquarks resemble gauge supermultiplet scalars (colour-neutral, integer electric charge). Treating B-L differently suggests skepticism toward R-symmetry. With colour neutrality, three chiral supermultiplets could be formed where one scalar becomes W/Z's extra degree of freedom and another surfaces as Higgs.

---

## Main Results/Conclusions

- The charge +4/3 diquark required by Tevatron anomaly explanations arises naturally and uniquely from flavour SU(5) symmetry applied to SUSY scalars.
- The construction works only for 3 generations with 5 light quarks (u, c, d, s, b).
- There is a potential Technicolor interpretation where these diquarks condense.

---

---

# Paper 3: arXiv:2407.05397

**Title:** "An interpretation of scalars in SO(32)"
**Author:** Alejandro Rivero
**Submitted:** July 7, 2024; revised July 24, 2024
**Published:** European Physical Journal C 84, 1058 (2024)
**Category:** hep-ph, 8 pages
**DOI:** https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2407.05397

---

## Abstract

"The paper proposes an interpretation for the adjoint representation of the SO(32) group to classify the scalars of a generic Supersymmetric Standard Model having just three generations of particles, via a flavour group SU(5)." The interpretation emerges from a self-consistency principle for scalar composites. The model uses only color and electric charge, and introduces an additional chiral +4/3 quark per generation.

---

## Section Structure

1. Introduction
2. The Flavour Group in SO(32)
3. SO(32) from Postulates (3.1 Turtles and Elephants; 3.2 Colourless and Coloured Flavour Groups)
4. A Case for One Generation
5. Discussion on Related Groups (5.1 SU(15); 5.2 SU(8); 5.3 E8 x E8)
6. Discussion
7. Conclusions
8. Acknowledgements

---

## Section 2: Group Decomposition

For SO(2n) groups with SU(3) color, with constraint $2n = n_1 + 6n_2$, the adjoint of SO(32) decomposes into 496 components:

```latex
496 = (1,24,1^c) + [1,15,\bar{3}^c] + [1,\bar{15},3^c] + (1,24,8^c) + \ldots
```

The three bold terms -- $(1,24,1^c)$, $[1,15,\bar{3}^c]$, $[1,\bar{15},3^c]$ -- provide scalars matching a minimal three-generation SUSY model.

SU(5) flavor decomposes under SU(3) x SU(2) x U_2(1):

```latex
15 = (1,3)_{-6} + (3,2)_{-1} + (6,1)_4
```

```latex
24 = (1,1)_0 + (1,3)_0 + (3,2)_5 + (\bar{3},2)_{-5} + (8,1)_0
```

Electric charge formula:

```latex
Q = \frac{1}{5}\left(\frac{2}{3}Y_1 - Y_2\right)
```

Alternative decomposition chain:

```latex
SO(32) \supset SU(16) \times U(1) \supset SU(15) \times U(1) \supset SU(5) \times SU(3)
```

with:

```latex
496 = 1_0 + 120_4 + \bar{120}_{-4} + 255_0
```

---

## Section 3.1: Turtles and Elephants

Scalars are treated as composites of quark pairs. With N generations, $k_u$ up-type "turtle" quarks and $k_d$ down-type "turtle" quarks, the consistency conditions for squarks are:

```latex
2N = k_u k_d  \quad (8)
```

```latex
2N = \frac{k_d(k_d+1)}{2}  \quad (9)
```

Adding slepton conditions:

```latex
4N = k_u^2 + k_d^2 - 1  \quad (11)
```

**Unique solution:** $N = 3$, $k_u = 2$, $k_d = 3$

Five "turtle" quarks (u, c, d, s, b) + one "elephant" quark (top) that does not participate in composites.

Three extra scalar quarks of charge $\pm 4/3$ emerge from all turtle combinations.

---

## Section 4: One-Generation Case (Georgi-Glashow)

```latex
5 = (1,2)_{-3} + (3,1)_2
```

```latex
10 = (1,1)_{-6} + (\bar{3},1)_4 + (3,2)_{-1}
```

The union $10 + \bar{10} + 24$ is the single-generation version, with X bosons appearing as +4/3 scalars.

---

## Section 6: Mass Formula

Classical preon mass formula: energy for pair $(q_a, q_b)$:

```latex
E(q_a, q_b) = (q_a + q_b)^2 K_\Omega   \quad (14)
```

For three-preon composites, two conditions:

```latex
z_1 + z_2 + z_3 = 0   \quad (15)
```

```latex
z_0^2 = \frac{z_1^2 + z_2^2 + z_3^2}{3}   \quad (16)
```

Angular parametrization of abelian charges from SU(3):

```latex
z_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}\cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\sin\alpha
```

```latex
z_2 = 0 - \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\sin\alpha
```

```latex
z_3 = -\tfrac{1}{2}\cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\sin\alpha
```

```latex
z_0 = \pm\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{6}}
```

For $\alpha = 0.745821$, the formula recovers exactly $m_e, m_\mu, m_\tau$ using $k = m_e + m_\mu + m_\tau$.

At $\alpha = 0$: exact mass pairs throughout all representations (unbroken SUSY condition).

Squark mass analysis gives:

```
u = c = 313.85,  s = 0,  d = b = 470.8
```

Producing: $(u,c,t) = (0, 470.8, 1883)$ and $(d,s,b) = (15.8, 313.85, 1553.4)$ MeV.

---

## Conclusions

1. SO(32)'s adjoint representation naturally accommodates three-generation SUSY scalar content.
2. A self-consistency ("turtles all the way down") postulate uniquely determines $N=3$.
3. The decomposition identifies five "light" turtle quarks and one heavy elephant (top).
4. Classical mass formulas produce equal-mass pairs required by unbroken SUSY.
5. Each generation produces two extra $\pm 4/3$ scalar quarks -- phenomenologically exotic but theoretically necessary.
6. "Supersymmetry could be hiding in plain sight, not broken but distorted."
7. Related groups SO(30), SU(15), SU(8), E8 x E8 are discussed but none achieve the same elegance.

---

---

# Paper 4: arXiv:hep-ph/0606171

**Title:** "Mass terms to break susy-like degeneration"
**Author:** Alejandro Rivero (EUPT, University of Zaragoza)
**Submitted:** June 15, 2006
**Category:** hep-ph, 6 pages
**DOI:** https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.hep-ph/0606171

---

## Abstract

"A very simple but general operator to break mass degeneration between representations of the Poincare group having spin 1 and 1/2." During this process, a quantity resembling Weinberg's angle emerges, matching experimental values within 0.13 standard deviations.

---

## Section Structure

1. Mass Terms from Casimir Invariants
2. Breaking SUSY Multiplet Degeneracy
3. De Broglie's 1924 Relativistic Quantum Mechanics (de Vries' angle)
4. Implications / Additional Numerical Coincidences

---

## Section 1: Mass Terms from Casimir Invariants

Mass operator $M_s^2$ is constructed from Poincare Casimir invariants $\mathcal{C}_1$ and $\mathcal{C}_2$ with eigenvalues $c_1 = m^2$ and $c_2 = -m^2 s(s+1)$.

Asymptotic constraint (preserving Regge trajectories): $\lim_{s \to \infty} m_s^2 = m$.

The simplest solution satisfying this:

```latex
M_s^4 - M_s^2 \mathcal{C}_2 + \mathcal{C}_1 \mathcal{C}_2 = 0
```

Using Pauli matrices to avoid square roots:

```latex
M_s^2 = \sigma^+ \otimes \mathcal{C}_1 \mathcal{C}_2 + \sigma^- \otimes \mathbf{I} + \frac{\mathbf{I}-\sigma_z}{2} \otimes \mathcal{C}_2
```

---

## Central Finding: de Vries' Angle

Hans de Vries discovered that positive eigenvalues for $s=1/2$ and $s=1$ yield:

```latex
s_{dV}^2 \equiv 1 - \frac{m_{s=1/2,+}^2}{m_{s=1,+}^2} = 0.22310132\ldots
```

The electroweak Weinberg angle:

```latex
s_W^2 = 1 - \frac{M_W^2}{M_Z^2} = 0.22306 \pm 0.00033
```

Agreement ratio: $s_{dV}^2 / s_W^2 = 0.9998 \pm 0.0015$ (0.13 sigma).

---

## Section 2: Breaking SUSY Multiplet Degeneracy

For degenerate mass $(m, s_i)$ SUSY multiplet representations, the operator:

```latex
M_{(s)}^2 = \frac{1}{2}\left(\mathcal{C}_2 + \sqrt{(\mathcal{C}_2)^2 - 4\mathcal{C}_1 \mathcal{C}_2}\right)
```

breaks degeneracy while maintaining asymptotic Regge behavior.

Using $M_Z^2 = (91.1874\,\text{GeV})^2$ as input:

- $m_{s=1/2,+}^2 = (80.3717\,\text{GeV})^2$ (near $M_W$)
- $m_{s=1,-}^2 = -(176.154\,\text{GeV})^2$
- $m_{s=1/2,-}^2 = -(122.384\,\text{GeV})^2$

The vacuum expectation value: $\langle v \rangle / \sqrt{2} = 174.1042 \pm 0.00075$ GeV, with coupling $\lambda_h \approx 1$.

---

## Section 3: De Broglie / de Vries Connection

De Vries combined de Broglie's relativistic orbit rule with the Lande-Pauli angular momentum substitution. Setting orbital period $T_r = h/(m_0 c^2)$:

```latex
\frac{\beta^2}{\sqrt{1-\beta^2}} = \sqrt{j(j+1)}
```

Solutions:

```latex
\beta_{1/2} = \sqrt{\frac{3}{8}(\sqrt{19/3}-1)} = 0.7541414\ldots
```

```latex
\beta_1 = \sqrt{\sqrt{3}-1} = 0.8555996\ldots
```

```latex
s_{dV}^2 \equiv 1 - \left(\frac{\beta_{1/2}}{\beta_1}\right)^2 = 0.22310132\ldots
```

---

## Additional Numerical Coincidences

```latex
\cos\theta = \sinh^{-1}(1) - s_W^2 = 0.2231806
```

```latex
\sin\theta/\cos\theta = \beta_1^4 - s_W^2 = 0.223112151
```

---

## Main Results/Conclusions

- A Casimir-based mass operator naturally splits spin-1 from spin-1/2 masses.
- The ratio of the resulting masses reproduces the Weinberg angle (sin^2 theta_W) to 0.13 sigma from pure Poincare group theory.
- The splitting has a classical relativistic mechanics interpretation via de Broglie/de Vries.
- The top Yukawa coupling $\lambda_t = 0.991 \pm 0.013$ aligns with these calculations.
- "The electroweak scale may be defined as the point where the running Weinberg angle reaches de Vries' value."

---

---

# Paper 5: viXra:1509.0090

**Title:** "Possible Sensibility of Nuclear Fragmentation to the Mass of W"
**Author:** Alejandro Rivero
**Submitted:** September 7, 2015
**Category:** Nuclear and Atomic Physics, viXra
**Length:** 6 pages (draft note with graphical updates)
**Downloads:** 182 unique-IP

---

## Abstract

"The possibility of an enhancement of the yields of asymmetric fission when the mass of one of the fragments has the same size that the electroweak W mass, causing some kind of recoil resonance."

---

## Main Idea

The paper examines whether nuclear fission yield distributions show an enhancement when one fragment's mass (in atomic mass units, near ~80 u) coincides with the W boson mass (~80.4 GeV/c^2). The proposal is a resonance-type effect: a "recoil resonance" when the fragment mass numerically matches the W boson mass scale.

---

## Main Results/Conclusions

- Investigates a possible anomalous enhancement in asymmetric fission yields near the W-mass coincidence.
- Presents graphical evidence for or against the enhancement.
- The paper is a preliminary note/draft and the result is presented as speculative/exploratory.
- This connects nuclear physics observables to electroweak boson masses -- an unconventional interdisciplinary conjecture.

(Full equations and detailed data were not extractable from the PDF binary; the above reflects all text visible on the abstract page and metadata.)

---

# Paper 6: viXra:1901.0074

**Title:** "Up to SO(32) Via Supersymmetry Bootstrap"
**Author:** Alejandro Rivero
**Submitted:** January 6, 2019
**Category:** High Energy Particle Physics, viXra
**Length:** "Three similar versions of 3-4 pages each, and a coda"
**Downloads:** 199 unique-IP

---

## Abstract

"A simple self-referencing postulate in the Chan-Paton factors of a string allows to fix some of the freedom in the landscape of SO(32) model aspiring to be standard-model-like."

---

## Main Idea

This is an earlier, multi-version draft predecessor to the published arXiv:2407.05397 paper. The paper applies the "supersymmetry bootstrap" (sBootstrap) self-referencing postulate -- that scalars are composites of quark pairs -- to the Chan-Paton factors of an open string theory, specifically the SO(32) heterotic/Type I string. This constrains the landscape of SO(32) vacua that are compatible with the Standard Model.

The "coda" structure and three similar versions suggest iterative refinement of the argument across the document.

---

## Key Connection to arXiv:2407.05397

The self-consistency postulate used here ("turtles all the way down") is the same one fully developed in the 2024 EPJ C publication:

```latex
2N = k_u k_d,  \quad 2N = \frac{k_d(k_d+1)}{2},  \quad 4N = k_u^2 + k_d^2 - 1
```

with unique solution N=3, k_u=2, k_d=3.

---

## Main Results/Conclusions

- A self-referential Chan-Paton postulate picks out specific SO(32) models as Standard-Model-like.
- The bootstrap uniquely selects 3 generations.
- This is preliminary/exploratory work later fully developed in the 2024 EPJC publication.

---

---

# Paper 7: viXra:1102.0034

**Title:** "No Elementary Scalars in Experimental Supersymmetry"
**Author:** Alejandro Rivero
**Submitted:** February 20, 2011
**Category:** High Energy Particle Physics, viXra
**Length:** 2 pages
**Downloads:** 280 unique-IP

---

## Abstract

"This sheet presents an extreme interpretation of the global SU(5) symmetry that has been gradually discovered in the spectrum of scalar particles of the Supersymmetric Standard Model. It postulates that such scalars are actually the different aspects of the QCD string. If so, only the gauginos and perhaps two neutral Higgs particles are candidates for discovery in the LHC."

---

## Main Idea

This is an early, compact statement of the sBootstrap program. The scalars of the MSSM (squarks and sleptons) are not elementary but are identified with different configurations of the QCD string. The SU(5) flavour symmetry observed in their quantum numbers is explained because they are composites of the five light quarks (u, c, d, s, b).

---

## Key Claim

Under this interpretation:
- No elementary scalar superpartners exist or will be found at the LHC.
- Only **gauginos** (fermionic partners of gauge bosons) and possibly **two neutral Higgs bosons** are genuine new particles discoverable at the LHC.
- All other "SUSY scalars" in the spectrum are reinterpreted as QCD string states organized by global SU(5).

---

## Main Results/Conclusions

- Proposes the most radical form of the sBootstrap: SUSY is "experimental" in the sense that the scalar degrees of freedom already exist in QCD, not as new particles.
- Directly predicts the LHC will not find squarks or sleptons as new particles.
- The SU(5) flavour symmetry of MSSM scalars is the direct consequence of them being composites.
- Written in February 2011, shortly before LHC exclusions of simple MSSM began to mount.

---

---
